cover_story.headline: Naomi Osaka Built a Skincare Brand Athletes Actually Need
cover_story.dek: Kinlò just closed an $18M Series A — and Osaka's bet on melanin-rich skin is turning into one of the sharpest founder plays in athlete CPG.
cover_story.author: Marcus Webb
body: Naomi Osaka is a four-time Grand Slam champion. She's also a founder. In 2021 she launched Kinlò, a sunscreen and skincare line built specifically for people with melanin-rich skin — a category that major beauty brands had systematically ignored. The product line started with SPF essentials and has since expanded into moisturizers, lip balms, and body care.

Kinlò just closed an $18M Series A led by Fearless Fund, with participation from CAVU Consumer Partners and a handful of athlete angel investors. The brand has moved 600,000+ units to date and is now stocked in Target, Ulta Beauty, and Amazon — a retail trifecta that most indie beauty brands spend a decade chasing.
Next up: a hair care line launching in Q3 and an ambassador program specifically targeting college athletes in Olympic sports. Osaka is targeting $30M in revenue by end of 2026.
cover_story.quote: I didn't want to be the face of someone else's sunscreen. I wanted to solve a real problem for people who look like me — and own the upside.

side_stories.headline: Patrick Mahomes' Restaurant Group Expands to 12 Locations
side_stories.body: The Chiefs QB's casual dining brand, 1587 Prime, has signed leases in four new cities following record same-store sales in 2025. Mahomes holds equity across all locations — no licensing fee model.

side_stories.headline: Jaylen Brown Launches Mental Performance App for High Schoolers
side_stories.body: Brown's app, Clearview, hit 200,000 downloads in its first 90 days — targeting student athletes with guided mindset and recovery protocols. A $6M seed round closed last month.

side_stories.headline: Being an Ambassador Is a Job. Being a Founder Is a Career.
side_stories.body: Every year athletes leave billions on the table by choosing spokesperson deals over equity stakes. The athletes who will be wealthy at 60 are the ones building right now — not the ones collecting flat fees.

side_stories.headline: Richie McCaw's Leadership Consultancy Crosses $10M Revenue
side_stories.body: The All Blacks legend turned corporate performance consultant is running leadership programs for Fortune 500 executives — and the business is scaling faster than any sports deal he ever signed.

roundup.headline: Rory McIlroy Takes Stake in Golf Analytics Firm
roundup.body: McIlroy invested an undisclosed sum in SwingIQ, a machine-learning platform that provides real-time biomechanical feedback to tour players and coaches. The company's B2B product is now deployed at 200 training facilities across North America and Europe, with a consumer app launching in Q4.

roundup.headline: Megan Rapinoe's Re-Inc Brand Expands to 80 New Retail Doors
roundup.body: Rapinoe's gender-fluid apparel brand Re-Inc has added 80 new retail doors in 2026, including a flagship deal with Nordstrom. The brand's community-first model drives repeat purchase rates nearly double the industry average, according to company disclosures.

roundup.headline: Candace Parker Co-Leads $25M Raise for Women's Sports Media Platform
roundup.body: Parker co-led a Series B for Togethxr, the media company she co-founded with Alex Morgan, Chloe Kim, and Sue Bird. The platform is expanding into live event ticketing and merchandise — two categories where women's sports demand has consistently outpaced supply.

roundup.headline: Sydney McLaughlin-Levrone Takes Equity in Recovery Startup
roundup.body: The 400m hurdles world record holder has negotiated a 2% equity stake in RecovR, a wearable-based recovery monitoring platform. The deal includes a standard ambassador component, but McLaughlin-Levrone insisted the equity grant was non-negotiable — and got it.

tips.headline: Know the difference between revenue and income
tips.body: Revenue is what your business makes. Income is what you keep. Build systems that maximize the gap between the two before you scale.

tips.headline: Your athlete status is a distribution moat
tips.body: No startup founder gets the access and attention you do at launch. Use your visibility as a customer acquisition asset — but build the product to retain them.

tips.headline: Retail is a trap without margin control
tips.body: Getting into Target feels like winning. But if your margins aren't protected, volume kills you. Know your cost of goods before you negotiate shelf space.

tips.headline: Hire operators, not cheerleaders
tips.body: Your first hires should have operational track records — not just excitement about working with you. Enthusiasm runs out. Systems scale.

tips.headline: Separate playing income from business capital
tips.body: Never fund your company from your playing salary without a formal structure. Set up a holding company and treat every dollar like an investor would.

tips.headline: Build the community before you build the product
tips.body: If you have an audience, survey them before you spend a dollar on development. The product they'll pay for is rarely the product you'd build in isolation.

tips.headline: Think in decades, not deal cycles
tips.body: A 10-year brand is worth exponentially more than a 10-year contract. Start building the thing that compounds while your peers are still chasing endorsement checks.

investments.headline: Lagos Basketball Academy Gets Seed Funding
investments.body: Giannis committed $4M to a Lagos-based basketball development academy that scouts and trains athletes across sub-Saharan Africa. The investment is part of a broader initiative to build talent pipeline infrastructure in regions chronically underserved by traditional scouting networks.

investments.headline: AI Legal Platform Built for Professional Athletes
investments.body: Serena Ventures led a seed round for Playbook Legal, a platform helping professional athletes review contracts, negotiate endorsement deals, and understand IP ownership without six-figure legal fees. The platform has already onboarded 400 active professional athletes across six sports.

investments.headline: Franchise Restaurant Digital Ordering Platform
investments.body: O'Neal participated in a Series B for OLIO Tech, a white-label digital ordering and loyalty platform built for franchise restaurant groups. Shaq has operated in the QSR space for over a decade and the investment deepens his vertical integration in food service operations.
